revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:切り合い関係]]


{{merge|Cross-cutting relationships}}

'''Intercutting Relationships''' (切り合い関係) is a term primarily used in {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|qid=Q23498|lt=Archaeology|1=Archaeology}} to describe evidence of overlapping traces. While "superimposition" (累重) refers to the overlapping of positive traces, "kiriai" pertains to the overlapping of negative traces.<ref name="da2arc" />{{refnest|group="note"|Superimposition, or "ruiju," can also be applied to infer production processes through the overlapping of positive traces, such as clay coils in pottery-making or brushstrokes in calligraphy.<ref name="da2arc">[https://2nd-archaeology.blog.ss-blog.jp/2006-08-17 Archaeological Chronology Theory (Summary 6): Trace Research]</ref>}}.

== Intercutting in Archaeological Features ==
{{See also|Stratigraphy (archaeology)|Relative dating}}
[[File:Relation1010.svg|thumb|220px|right|The white pit is "cut" by the black pit, indicating the white pit is older.]]
Intercutting relationships describe the chronological order of overlapping [[:en:Archaeological_features|archaeological features]] constructed during different periods. This term is used on archaeological sites to determine stratigraphic relationships of newer and older features.<ref name="keyword56">[[#Keyword|Archaeology Keywords (1997), pp. 56-57]]</ref> For example, if feature B cuts through feature A, it is evident that feature B is newer than feature A.<ref name="keyword56"/> This allows archaeologists to understand the construction and abandonment sequence of {{ill|dwelling|en|dwelling|qid=Q699405|lt=dwellings|1=Dwelling}} or {{ill|Prehistoric_storage_pits|en|Prehistoric_storage_pits|qid=Q1421012|lt=pits|1=Prehistoric storage pits}} in a given layer or surface.<ref name="keyword56"/>

In the case of pits, the sequence typically follows a cycle: formation of the initial pit (negative trace), deposition within the pit (positive trace), creation of a subsequent pit (negative trace), and deposition within the new pit (positive trace). The older pit, being "cut" by the newer one, is judged to be the earlier feature.<ref name="da2arc" />

== Intercutting in Artefacts ==
The methods of trace research used in features are also applied to {{ill|Artifact_(archaeology)|en|Artifact_(archaeology)|qid=Q220659|lt=artefacts|1=Artifact (archaeology)}} with significant results.

=== Intercutting in Stone Tools ===
Observing {{ill|Stone_tool|en|Stone_tool|qid=Q479257|lt=stone tools|1=Stone tool}} reveals that when multiple flake surfaces overlap on the same rock ([[:en:core (archaeology)|core]]), the sequence of flaking can be determined by examining the [[ring crack|rings]] (impact marks). The order of flake formation (negative traces) allows researchers to reconstruct the manufacturing process of the tool.<ref name="da2arc" />

=== Intercutting in Pottery ===
For {{ill|Pottery|en|Pottery|qid=Q11642|lt=Pottery|1=Pottery}}, particularly [[:en:Jomon_pottery|Jomon pottery]], overlapping decorative patterns can indicate the sequence of application. This technique can also extend to other fields, such as observing intersecting incised lines in {{ill|Molding_(process)|en|Molding_(process)|qid=Q1055732|lt=metal moulds|1=Molding (process)}} or overlapping scratches in [[kofun]] wall paintings. Akira Igarashi proposed this as a versatile method applicable to a wide range of subjects.<ref name="da2arc" />
